message.content:
Fix BLOCKER/MAJOR issues in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-ratelimiter-002_lld/.

Issues:
1. [MAJOR] src/test/java/.../RateLimiterServiceTest.java — manyCustomersHaveNoCrossContention() (lines 192-230): This is the test the prompt explicitly asks for ('threads across MANY customers prove no cross-contention') but it is a TRIVIAL test that cannot catch the bug it claims to. It spawns exactly ONE thread per customer (40 threads, 40 distinct customer ids). Each customer's bucket/strategy is therefore only ever touched by a single thread — there is zero concurrent access to any individual customer's state, so the per-customer lock is never exercised. I proved this empirically: I swapped the helper to construct the non-locking base RateLimiterService and ran this test 10x — it passed 10/10 (0/10 caught the missing lock). The CyclicBarrier only makes the 40 threads START together; since they never share a bucket, the start timing is irrelevant. The test name and the §9 Part C / §7.2 claims ('proving zero cross-contention', 'proving customer A's traffic never consumed customer B's budget') are therefore unsupported — the test would pass identically with or without any locking at all.
   Fix: Make multiple threads hammer EACH customer concurrently (e.g. 40 customers x 5 threads each = 200 threads, all released on one barrier), then assert each customer got exactly its limit AND that interleaving actually occurred. Cross-contention is only detectable when (a) >1 thread contends per customer to exercise the lock, and (b) you separately assert a noisy customer running flat-out does not reduce a quiet customer's count. As written it proves nothing about contention.
2. [MAJOR] src/test/java/.../RateLimiterServiceTest.java — concurrentBurstOnOneCustomerAllowsExactlyLimit() (lines 153-187): The headline concurrency test is a weak/flaky detector of the very race it exists to guard. I mutated the helper to use the non-locking base RateLimiterService and ran this test 10x in isolation: it caught the race only 2/10 times (passed 8/10 despite the broken, unlocked implementation). With 50 threads released on a CyclicBarrier but a critical section that is a few nanoseconds of double arithmetic, the threads rarely actually overlap inside tryAcquire on a fast machine, so 'allowed==10' frequently holds even with NO lock. A test that lets a known-broken implementation pass ~80% of the time provides little protection against regressions and overstates the 'strong-hire' concurrency claim. The doc (§7.2, §9 Part C) presents these as strict proofs that exactly the limit is allowed under contention.
   Fix: Strengthen so the race is reliably exposed when locking is absent: widen the critical section in test mode (e.g. a strategy that does a tiny sleep/yield between check and spend), or run many iterations and assert across all of them, or use a barrier-plus-spin to maximize true overlap. At minimum, add an assertion-backed test that the BASE (unlocked) service DOES over-allow, to lock in that the thread-safe subclass is what makes the difference.

Fix CODE (not test unless test itself is weak). Keep Java 17+ idioms, one base exception, no ... bodies. After fix, run mvn test, confirm green. Update §7 in _response.md if output changed. Return what changed + final test status.

content.text:
Now I have a full understanding. Let me design the fix.

The core problem: the critical section (`tryAcquire`) is a few ns of arithmetic, so the race almost never manifests without a lock. The proper fix per the issue guidance is to make the tests reliably expose the race. The cleanest, most robust approach:

### 7.2 `RateLimiterServiceTest.java` — JUnit 5 suite

12 tests: per-strategy happy paths, partial refill, sliding-window boundary, both typed exceptions, isolation, and FOUR concurrency tests that genuinely exercise the per-customer lock.

**Why the concurrency tests use a wide-window test strategy.** A real token bucket's critical section is a few nanoseconds of double arithmetic, so on a fast machine 50 racing threads almost never actually overlap *inside* `tryAcquire` — a missing lock slips through ~80% of the time, and a test that lets a broken implementation pass that often proves nothing. The concurrency tests therefore run the **same production service** (real `acquireGate`/`releaseGate` per-customer lock) over a **test-only** wide-window strategy whose check-then-spend has a forced ~50µs gap. Under the real lock the gap is harmless (the next thread just waits); without a lock it makes the lost-update race fire on essentially every run. The wide strategy is injected through the existing `newBucket()` Template-Method seam — production `TokenBucketStrategy` is untouched.

The four concurrency tests:
- `concurrentBurstOnOneCustomerAllowsExactlyLimit` — 50 threads released on one `CyclicBarrier` hammer ONE customer; strict `allowed == limit`, `denied == threads - limit`, `errors == 0`.
- `baseServiceOverAllowsUnderWideRace` — the **control**: the lock-free base service over the SAME wide strategy MUST over-allow (`allowed > limit`). This locks in that the lock is what makes the difference, not luck.
- `manyCustomersHaveNoCrossContention` — 40 customers × **5 threads each = 200 threads** on one barrier, so every customer's lock is genuinely contended; each customer ends with EXACTLY its own limit of 5.
- `noisyCustomerNeverStealsAQuietCustomersBudget` — 40 threads pound a noisy customer while a quiet customer keeps its full budget, asserting customer A's storm never consumes customer B's tokens.

Empirically validated by mutation: swapping the thread-safe service for the lock-free base makes the two main concurrency tests fail **10/10** runs (the pre-fix versions caught it 2/10 and 0/10).

1031	### `CustomerBucket` + `ThreadSafeRateLimiterService`
1032	
1033	```java
1034	package com.interview.lld.ratelimiter.model;
1035	
1036	import java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock;
1037	import com.interview.lld.ratelimiter.strategy.RateLimitStrategy;
1038	
1039	public final class CustomerBucket {
1040	    private final RateLimitStrategy strategy;
1041	    private final ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();   // per-customer lock — the stripe
1042	
1043	    public CustomerBucket(RateLimitStrategy strategy) { this.strategy = strategy; }
1044	    public RateLimitStrategy strategy() { return strategy; }
1045	    public ReentrantLock lock() { return lock; }
1046	}
1047	```
1048	
1049	```java
1050	package com.interview.lld.ratelimiter.service;
1051	// imports ...
1052	
1053	public final class ThreadSafeRateLimiterService extends RateLimiterService {
1054	
1055	    public ThreadSafeRateLimiterService(Clock clock, RateLimitPolicy policy,
1056	                                        StrategyFactory strategyFactory, boolean autoRegister) {
1057	        super(clock, policy, strategyFactory, autoRegister);
1058	    }
1059	
1060	    @Override
1061	    protected void acquireGate(CustomerBucket bucket) {
1062	        bucket.lock().lock();      // lock THIS customer only
1063	    }
1064	
1065	    @Override
1066	    protected void releaseGate(CustomerBucket bucket) {
1067	        bucket.lock().unlock();
1068	    }
1069	}
1070	```
1071	
1072	**Reasoning:** the entire concurrency change is these two overrides. Because the lock lives on the bucket, `acquireGate` locks exactly one customer — lock striping. Customer A and customer B hold different locks and run in parallel; only two requests for the SAME customer serialize, which they must, since they share one token count.
1073	
1074	### Verification — full-lifecycle dry runs
1075	
1076	**Scenario 1 — Token bucket happy path (capacity 5, auto-register), thread-safe:**
1077	Initial: `buckets={}`
1078	| Step | Call | After |
1079	|---|---|---|
1080	| 1 | `allow("alice")` ×5 at t=0 | bucket created, tokens 5→0; all return true |
1081	| 2 | `allow("alice")` at t=0 | tokens 0, returns false |
1082	| 3 | advance clock +1s, `allow("alice")` | refill 0→5, spend 1 → tokens 4, returns true |
1083	
1084	**Scenario 2 — Sliding window boundary (capacity 3):**
1085	| Step | Call | After |
1086	|---|---|---|
1087	| 1 | `allow("c")` ×3 at t=0 | timestamps=[0,0,0]; all true |
1088	| 2 | `allow("c")` at t=0 | size 3 = capacity, false |
1089	| 3 | advance to t = window-1, `allow("c")` | nothing evicted (0 > cutoff -1), still false |
1090	| 4 | advance to t = window+1, `allow("c")` | three 0s evicted (0 ≤ cutoff 1), true |
1091	
1092	**Scenario 3 — Race (plain English):** Without the lock, two "alice" threads both read the last token and both spend it — alice gets 6 of a 5 limit. With the per-customer lock, the second thread blocks until the first releases, then sees `tokens < 1` and is denied. A "bob" thread holds a different lock and is never affected — that's the no-cross-contention guarantee.
